explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled, adding unnecessary client and network traffic.
Transactions are used later, but SET XACT_ABORT is OFF, allowing some runtime errors to leave transactions partially applied.
SELECT * returns every current and future column from Sales.Orders and couples callers to the table schema.
TOP (10) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary set of orders.
OrderID is the primary key of Sales.Orders, so DISTINCT adds unnecessary duplicate-elimination work.
The NOLOCK hint can return dirty, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR is applied to the indexed OrderDate column in a predicate, preventing an efficient date-range seek.
The unlabelled year literal 2016 embeds date-selection logic directly in the query.
The int CustomerID column is compared with the nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText, which can cause an implicit conversion and impair index usage.
The optional-parameter OR predicate can produce poor cardinality estimates and prevent efficient seeking.
The leading wildcard in the Comments LIKE pattern prevents a normal index seek.
Wrapping Orders.CustomerID in ISNULL makes the predicate non-sargable.
Adding zero to Orders.OrderID in the predicate prevents direct index matching.
The <> predicate on CustomerID is generally non-selective and may require scanning many rows.
The business filter uses the embedded CustomerID value 1 instead of a parameter.
TOP (1) on the cross join has no ORDER BY, so the selected order/customer pair is arbitrary.
CROSS JOIN intentionally produces every combination of Orders and Customers, potentially multiplying the result set.
TOP (1) is used without ORDER BY, so the returned order is nondeterministic.
Comma-separated tables with the join condition in WHERE use legacy join syntax and obscure join semantics.
The correlated COUNT subquery performs a per-order lookup and may be less efficient than a grouped set-based join.
The correlated EXISTS check is evaluated for outer rows and could be refactored into a set-based join or precomputed key set.
The same CASE expression based on Comments is repeated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 depends on the select-list position and can silently change behavior when columns are reordered.
OFFSET/FETCH orders only by OrderDate and lacks a stable unique tie-breaker such as OrderID.
The branches filter for disjoint OrderID values, so UNION performs unnecessary duplicate elimination.
The query embeds business OrderID values 1 and 2 directly in separate predicates.
The query embeds business OrderID values 1 and 2 directly in separate predicates.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts matching order lines when EXISTS could stop at the first match.
SELECT assignment can process multiple rows and silently leave @OrderID set to the last value.
TOP (5) without ORDER BY inserts an arbitrary set of OrderID values into @OrderIds.
SELECT INTO implicitly creates #ImplicitOrders and couples its schema to the query projection.
TOP (5) without ORDER BY selects an arbitrary set of orders.
The cursor fetches rows one at a time and should be replaced with set-based processing where possible.
The cursor's TOP (2) query has no ORDER BY, so the fetched orders are arbitrary.
The WHILE loop performs iterative processing that may be replaceable with set-based logic.
The dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating @CustomerID into executable SQL.
EXEC executes dynamically constructed SQL without parameterized inputs.
User-controlled @CustomerID is concatenated into executable SQL, creating a SQL injection risk.
The dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating @OrderID instead of binding it as a parameter.
sp_executesql is called without parameter definitions or bound parameter values.
The explicitly created temporary table #Scratch is not dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block only selects the error message and neither rethrows nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; THROW provides modern error propagation semantics.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured application diagnostics.
The query embeds CustomerID value 1 instead of accepting it as a parameter.
The unlabelled date literal '20160101' embeds business date logic directly in the query.
The date string '01/02/2016' is converted without an explicit style and is session-format dependent.
COALESCE is applied to both join columns, which can inhibit normal index-based join optimization.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary order.
The self-join only retrieves the same OrderID and can duplicate rows unnecessarily.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary order.
Sorting by the nvarchar(max) Comments column can require expensive wide-row sorting.
SUM aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines fact-like table without a restrictive filter.
The row-level OrderID > 0 predicate should be applied in WHERE before grouping.
The aggregate without GROUP BY returns one row, making ORDER BY OrderCount unnecessary.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; separate update/insert logic is safer.
The UPDATE assigns Comments to its current value, causing unnecessary logging and locking.
DELETE removes every order line with Quantity = -1 rather than targeting a stable key.
The delete operation embeds the business quantity value -1 directly in its predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread and should generally be replaced with asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is not enclosed in a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
IDENTITY_INSERT changes session state and creates fragile identity-management coupling.
The system procedure is invoked as sp_who without a schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT changes session behavior and is not restored before the procedure ends.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories omits a column list, making the statement dependent on table column order.
The category identifier 99 is embedded directly in the INSERT instead of being supplied as a parameter or looked up.
INSERT INTO #DemoCustomers omits a column list, making it dependent on table column order.
The dynamic SQL string concatenates @CustomerName into the LIKE expression.
sp_executesql is called without parameterized inputs.
User-controlled @CustomerName is concatenated into executable dynamic SQL, creating a SQL injection risk.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and modifies every row in the temporary table.
The customer category value 1 is embedded directly in the UPDATE.
Filtering o.OrderDate in WHERE removes NULL-extended rows and effectively converts the LEFT JOIN to an inner join.
The unlabelled date literal '2016-01-01' embeds business date logic directly in the query.
Comparing DeliveryMethodID with NULL using = never evaluates true; use IS NULL instead.
The scalar user-defined function dbo.GetCustomerType is called once per selected customer and can force row-by-row execution.
The transactional CATCH block only returns an error message and neither rethrows nor returns a failure status.
The CATCH block follows a transaction but does not roll it back before handling the error.
The hard-coded INDEX hint forces a specific index and can become invalid or harmful as the schema and workload change.
The query contains three nested subquery levels and should be simplified with a CTE or another set-based structure.
The leading wildcard in the CustomerName LIKE pattern prevents a normal index seek.
SELECT * from the table-valued function exposes all returned columns and couples callers to its schema.
The Multi function appears to be a multi-statement table-valued function, which commonly has poor cardinality estimates compared with an inline TVF.
